channel.title: Critical Theory on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Critical Theory on emsenn.net

item.title: Epistemic Culture
item.description:
An epistemic culture is the entire apparatus through which a knowledge community produces and validates what it knows. Karin Knorr-Cetina introduced the concept in Epistemic Cultures: How the Sciences Make Knowledge (1999), based on ethnographic comparison of two sciences: high-energy physics at CERN and molecular biology in a leading laboratory. Her central claim: domains are defined by how they know, not what they know.

The two fields she studied differ on nearly every dimension of knowledge production. High-energy physics works through collaborations of thousands of researchers, operates massive shared detectors, reasons from theoretical predictions to experimental confirmation, treats the individual researcher as interchangeable, and produces papers with author lists longer than the text. Molecular biology works through small laboratory groups, employs benchtop instruments manipulated by individual researchers, reasons from experimental observation toward theoretical explanation, and publishes under a few named authors. These are not superficial differences in style. They are different machineries of knowing — different ways of constituting objects, different relationships between theory and experiment, different social structures for producing consensus.

item.title: Trading Zone
item.description:
A trading zone is a site where practitioners from different scientific subcultures coordinate their work despite holding incommensurable theories, methods, and standards. Peter Galison introduced the concept in Image and Logic: A Material Culture of Microphysics (1997), borrowing the term from anthropology’s studies of intercultural trade.

Galison’s historical case is twentieth-century physics, where three largely independent subcultures — theorists, experimentalists, and instrument builders — needed to exchange results without sharing conceptual frameworks. They did this by developing local languages of coordination. These languages begin as pidgins: stripped-down, limited-purpose codes sufficient for specific exchanges (e.g., agreeing on what counts as a signal in a detector, without agreeing on the underlying physics). Over time, sustained contact produces creoles: richer languages with their own grammar and expressive power, capable of generating new knowledge that neither parent culture could produce alone.
